title: The Peacemakers
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 10-13-24 -PM- The Peacemakers - Matthew 5_9 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The World's Answer to Peace: John Lennon's "Imagine"
subpoints: Rolling Stone magazine (2004) ranked "Imagine" the third greatest song of all time
Lennon's vision: peace through abolishing national identities and religion
This is the prevailing secular view — UN, European Union, political left
But peace cannot be achieved through global efforts to destroy nationalistic ideology and religious convictions
Peace can only be made through the Prince of Peace, Jesus Christ

outline.title: The Peacemaker Is the Son of God
subpoints: Jesus deliberately uses the word "sons" rather than the generic "children" — a legal term in Roman law meaning a male heir with full legal rights
This actually elevates women: in Christ, both men and women are adopted as legal heirs with all the rights of sons
The peacemaker is one who has been legally adopted by God through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ
As children of the God of Peace, we must resemble our Heavenly Father (D.A. Carson)

outline.title: The Peacemaker as Evangelist
subpoints: Our biggest problem is not that we don't get along — it is that we have sinned against God and he is at enmity with us
Sinclair Ferguson: Jesus is not speaking about the mere cessation of hostility among nations but about the cessation of hostility between men and God
Augustine: "Our hearts are restless till they find rest in you" — and that restlessness shows itself in conflicts between nations and people
The primary means of bringing peace to the world is evangelism
John Paton went to cannibals; we may be called to go to Muslims, Europeans, Chinese, or Americans

outline.title: Peacemakers Among Ourselves: Two Word Pictures
subpoints: The fourth vow of church membership: "Do you pledge to study the peace of the church?"
Ministers' vow: "I promise to be zealous and faithful in maintaining the truth of the gospel and the purity and peace of the church"
Sinclair Ferguson: Is it not strange that churches that most loudly proclaim allegiance to God's infallible word often experience the most disruption?
Peace as referee: let the peace of God rule in your hearts — Col. 3:15; peace blows the whistle on any action out of line
Peace as bond: Eph. 4:1-3 — John Owen's illustration of diverse sticks bound by a rope and carried home
To cut that bond is to cut the cord that Christ himself tied around us

summary: In this Sunday evening sermon on the Beatitudes, Pastor Tito Lyro expounds Matthew 5:9 — "Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God" — as a complement to the morning's message on peace as the third fruit of the Spirit. He begins by contrasting the world's answer to peace (John Lennon's "Imagine" — abolish nations and religion) with the Bible's answer: peace can only be made through the Prince of Peace, Jesus Christ, because our fundamental problem is not that we fail to get along, but that we have sinned against God and stand under his wrath. He then unpacks the significance of the title "sons of God" in a Roman legal context, showing that it refers to legal adoption with full inheritance rights — which means that in Christ's kingdom, men and women alike are adopted as heirs of God, a claim that actually elevates women. The peacemaker is therefore first an evangelist, because evangelism is the way of peace: the primary means of bringing peace to any conflict — including the Middle East — is the preaching of the gospel of Jesus Christ. The sermon then turns to peacemaking within the church, with two word pictures drawn from Colossians 3:15 and Ephesians 4:1-3: peace as the referee that blows the whistle on anything out of line, and peace as the rope that binds diverse members into one bundle that Christ carries home. The sermon ends with a personal challenge: identify where you have been a peace-breaker and go make peace.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading

For our scripture reading, we are going to read one verse out of Matthew chapter 5, verse 9. Wait a second because it is just one verse and you might miss it.

*Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God.*

Let us pray together. Father, we thank you for your word. We pray, Father, that your word would have its way with us during this afternoon, that your Spirit would equip us to be peacemakers. In Jesus' name, Amen.

## The World's Answer to Peace

In 2004, Rolling Stone magazine ranked the 1971 John Lennon song "Imagine" as the number three greatest song of all time. The top two, for those keeping score, were "Like a Rolling Stone" by Bob Dylan at number one and "I Can't Get No Satisfaction" by the Rolling Stones at number two.

Lennon's song says this: *Imagine there's no countries. It isn't hard to do. Nothing to kill or die for. And no religion too. Imagine all the people living life in peace.* In an interview, Lennon was asked what he meant by the song. He answered: *It's not a new message: give peace a chance. We are not being unreasonable. Just saying give it a chance.* According to Lennon, peace comes to humanity by the abolition of national identities and the abolition of religion.

This seems to be the prevailing view of our world today — you see it in the United Nations, the European Union, and the political left. Yet peace cannot be made or achieved through global efforts to destroy nationalistic ideology and religious convictions. Peace can only be made or achieved through the Prince of Peace, the Lord Jesus Christ.

Our biggest problem isn't that we don't get along with one another. Our biggest problem is that we have sinned against God and he is in enmity with us.

## The Peacemaker Is the Son of God

In our passage the Lord Jesus says: *Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God.* This raises the question: who is the peacemaker?

Notice that the peacemaker is called a *son* of God. I used to think the word *sons* here was a generic word meaning children of God. But the more I have studied that expression in the New Testament, the more I have come to believe that Jesus did not mean to use *sons* in the generic sense. He used the word for *children* all over the place, but here he chooses to use the word *sons* deliberately.

Before we get alarmed at this, notice that this interpretation actually **elevates** the place of women in the thinking of Jesus Christ. In first-century Roman law, only males could be legally adopted and receive the name of the adopted father. A son was a legal heir with full inheritance rights. So here, Jesus is saying that even women, in this legal sense, are sons of God — adopted with all the legal rights of those who belong to the family of God. Jesus is saying that in his kingdom, men and women are adopted by the Father in a way that surpasses anything ever heard before. Both are legal heirs; both bear the name of God.

The peacemaker, then, is the one who has been legally adopted by God and brought into his family. As we saw this morning, God is described throughout the New Testament as the God of Peace. And as children of the God of Peace, we must resemble our Heavenly Father. D.A. Carson, in his commentary on Matthew, says: *The title "sons" belongs to the heirs of the kingdom who, meek and poor in spirit, loving righteousness yet merciful, are especially equipped for peacemaking and so reflect something of their Heavenly Father's character.*

It is Christ — through the blood of the everlasting covenant — who adopts you and causes you to be a peacemaker. Hebrews 13:20-21: *Now may the God of Peace, who brought up our Lord Jesus from the dead, that great Shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant, make you complete in every good work to do his will, working in you what is well-pleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ.*

## The Peacemaker as Evangelist

We have found peace with God through Jesus Christ. Now we will become not only messengers of peace but practitioners of that peace, called to bring peace to the world.

The primary means to bring peace to the world is not through treaties and UN resolutions — UN resolutions mean absolutely nothing. The primary means is the **evangelizing of the nations.** If you are truly a worker for peace, you will be working at evangelizing all peoples.

Sinclair Ferguson, in his commentary on the Sermon on the Mount, says: *Jesus is not speaking about the mere cessation of hostility among nations. He is speaking about the cessation of hostility between men and God. This is the peace he came to establish.* Augustine in the first book of his Confessions writes: *Our hearts are restless till they find rest in you* — and that restlessness shows itself in conflicts between nations and between people. The solution is finding that rest in God himself.

The peacemaker is, first and foremost, an evangelist, because evangelism is the way of peace. Take all the fighting in the Middle East. Though we appreciate the men and women of our armed forces who sacrifice their lives for our country, the ultimate solution is for you and me to spend our lives preaching the gospel of Jesus Christ in the midst of hostile peoples.

We learned from Elder Anderson this morning about John Paton, who was warned not to go to the New Hebrides because he might be eaten by cannibals — and who went anyway. The ultimate solution for peace there was for Paton to spend his life preaching the gospel in the midst of a hostile people.

The last means of peacemaking is for you and me to give up our sons and daughters to go and die for the cause of Christ among the Muslims, Europeans, Chinese, or Americans. When our children express a desire to go to places where perhaps death might be certain, our reaction cannot be *no, you're too precious to me* — but to give them to Christ, who is the sovereign Lord of the universe, who has numbered our days and knows exactly what the days of our children are.

It is Christ the Prince of Peace who rules the nations through his church. It is Christ who will cause the lion to lie down with the lamb. It is Christ who will turn swords into plowshares. And right now he is doing that through the preaching of the gospel — right here, as we consider his Word, and through you later today, as you tell your children and your co-workers about the God you worship.

## Peacemakers Among Ourselves

We are also called to be peacemakers among ourselves. James 3:17 says: *The wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceable.* And verse 18: *The fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace.* So the children of God will seek peace among themselves in the family of God — the church.

To promote peace in the church is one of the things we promise to do when we become members. The fourth vow of church membership is: *Do you pledge to study the peace of the church in areas where you disagree with the doctrine and standards of this church?* And pastors take a similar vow: *I promise to be zealous and faithful in maintaining the truth of the gospel and the purity and peace of the church, whatever persecution or opposition may arise against me on that account.*

Sinclair Ferguson asks: *Is it not strange that the churches that experience a great deal of disruption are often those that most loudly proclaim allegiance to God's infallible Word?* May that not be us. May we be faithful followers of the Word of God — and also people of peace.

Let me give you two word pictures that illustrate Jesus' teaching on being peacemakers among ourselves.

**First:** Peace is the referee who blows the whistle on any action that is out of line. Colossians 3:15: *Let the peace of God rule in your hearts.* Peace — the harmony and well-being of our fellowship — must be as important to us as its purity. We are not going to give up purity for peace, nor peace for purity. Both must be present. This means we submit our will, our position, our natural desires to the well-being of our fellowship.

Peacemaking is not avoiding conflict. It is not forcing people to agree with you by attacking or bullying them. Peacemaking is dealing with all conflicts that arise in the church and in our relationships in a biblical, God-glorifying way. It may include confrontation, admonition, even putting someone out of the church — but for the sake of peace that glorifies God.

**Second:** Peace is the bond or cord that ties us all together. Ephesians 4:1-3: *Endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace.* By nature and by instinct, we would never act as one body. But God's peace can accomplish that.

John Owen gives this illustration: A man collecting wood for his fireplace finds a good supply of branches of varied shapes and sizes — some long and thin, others short and thick, some straight, others twisted. He binds them together with a rope and in one bundle easily carries them home. That is what Christ does for us in the bond of peace. We are a varied bunch. Christ ties us together with the bond of peace. If you cut that bond, you cut the cord that Christ himself tied around us.

## Conclusion

Brothers and sisters, let me challenge you to examine your heart and consider where you have been guilty of peace-breaking instead of peacemaking. Is there a fellow child of God — a brother or sister — with whom you must make peace? Go to them. Take Paul's exhortation seriously: *As much as is within you, live at peace with all people.* Gain a brother back. Gain a sister back.

May we all seek the peace of Christ's church, as he seeks the peace of his church.

Father, thank you that you are the God of peace. We pray that you help us to be peacemakers as you call us to be. In Jesus' name, Amen.
